- commit-message: Generate concise git commit messages for Data Engineering changes. Use PROACTIVELY when the user asks to commit files, create a commit, or generate a git commit message.
- datalake-ci-permissions: Use when user asks to add or update any AWS resource deployment or GitLab CI IAM roles scoped to the datalake environment. Do not trigger for non-datalake environments (dev, prod, sandbox, services) unless explicitly requested.
- mr-description: Writes merge request message description when raising a GitLab Merge Request. Use when creating a merge request or when user asks to summarise changes for a merge request.
- sam-template: Use whenever an AWS SAM template.yaml is being created or modified in the dns-de-automation lambda style, or any SAM template that integrates with serverless-access-manager. Trigger on direct requests ("add a new lambda", "update template.yaml", "add an SQS target to the access manager"), AND proactively whenever Claude is implementing code changes that end up adding or modifying a SAM template.yaml as a side-effect (for example: adding a new Lambda env var, wiring a new event source, adding an IAM permission, introducing a new SAM resource). If the implementation touches template.yaml at all, invoke this skill before writing the change.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.thinking: The user wants to know what the reporter lambda response looks like. They have the file open in the IDE. Let me read it.

content.content:
1	import json
2	
3	from aws_lambda_powertools import Logger
4	from aws_lambda_powertools.tracing import Tracer
5	from aws_lambda_powertools.utilities.typing import LambdaContext
6	from dotenv import load_dotenv
7	
8	from dns_de_rte_orchestrator.audit_logging import (
9	    FallbackAuditLogReader,
10	    get_audit_log_reader,
11	)
12	
13	# Load .env file, mostly useful for testing
14	_ = load_dotenv()
15	
16	logger = Logger()
17	tracer = Tracer()  # Sets service via POWERTOOLS_SERVICE_NAME env var
18	
19	reader: FallbackAuditLogReader = get_audit_log_reader()
20	
21	
22	def _response(
23	    status_code: int,
24	    body: dict[str, object],
25	) -> dict[str, object]:
26	    return {
27	        "statusCode": status_code,
28	        "headers": {"Content-Type": "application/json"},
29	        "body": json.dumps(body),
30	    }
31	
32	
33	@tracer.capture_lambda_handler
34	@logger.inject_lambda_context
35	def lambda_handler(
36	    event: dict[str, object],
37	    _context: LambdaContext,
38	) -> dict[str, object]:
39	    """Entrypoint for AWS Lambda that retrieves the audit log for an erasure id."""
40	    raw_params: object = event.get("pathParameters")
41	    path_parameters: dict[str, object] = (  # pyright: ignore[reportUnknownVariableType]
42	        raw_params if isinstance(raw_params, dict) else {}
43	    )
44	    erasure_id: object = path_parameters.get("id")
45	
46	    if not isinstance(erasure_id, str) or not erasure_id.strip():
47	        return _response(400, {"error": "missing erasure id"})
48	
49	    try:
50	        entries = reader.get_logs(erasure_id)
51	    except Exception:
52	        logger.exception("Failed to retrieve audit log for %s", erasure_id)
53	        return _response(500, {"error": "internal error"})
54	
55	    if not entries:
56	        return _response(404, {"error": "no audit log found", "id": erasure_id})
57	
58	    return _response(
59	        200,
60	        {
61	            "id": erasure_id,
62	            "entries": [entry.to_json() for entry in entries],
63	        },
64	    )
65

content.text:
The response is a standard API Gateway proxy response shape:

- **400** — `{"error": "missing erasure id"}` when `pathParameters.id` is absent or blank
- **404** — `{"error": "no audit log found", "id": "<erasure_id>"}` when no entries exist
- **500** — `{"error": "internal error"}` on any exception from the log reader
- **200** — `{"id": "<erasure_id>", "entries": [...]}` where each entry is whatever `AuditLogEntry.to_json()` returns

All responses share the same envelope: `statusCode`, `headers: {"Content-Type": "application/json"}`, and a JSON-serialized `body` string.

I now have everything needed. The processor_lambda has no API events (it's not triggered by the API and likely runs on a schedule or manual invocation). Let me provide the complete report.

## Exploration Report: REST API and Reporter Lambda Wiring

File analyzed: `/Users/saurabhtyagi/Documents/workspace/dns-de-rte-orchestrator/template.yaml`

### 1. `CreateRequestApi` (AWS::Serverless::Api)

Defined at lines 54-75:

```yaml
CreateRequestApi:
  Type: AWS::Serverless::Api
  Properties:
    StageName: !Ref Environment
    OpenApiVersion: '2.0'
    Description: !Sub "RTE orchestrator create-request API (${Environment})"
    EndpointConfiguration:
      Type: PRIVATE
      VPCEndpointIds:
        - !Ref VPCEndpointId
    Auth:
      ApiKeyRequired: true
      ResourcePolicy:
        CustomStatements:
          - Effect: Allow
            Action: execute-api:Invoke
            Resource:
              - !Sub "arn:aws:execute-api:${AWS::Region}:${AWS::AccountId}:*"
            Principal: '*'
            Condition:
              StringEquals:
                aws:SourceVpce: !Ref VPCEndpointId
```

Key points:
- Stage config: `StageName` = the `Environment` parameter. No explicit `StageDescription`, `MethodSettings`, throttling, caching, or access-logging configured.
- Auth config: `ApiKeyRequired: true` is set at the API level (applies as the default for methods). There is no Lambda/Cognito authorizer — only API key.
- Endpoint type: PRIVATE, locked to a single VPC endpoint (`VPCEndpointId` parameter). A resource policy restricts `execute-api:Invoke` to requests arriving via that VPC endpoint (`aws:SourceVpce` condition). The API is not reachable from the public internet.
- Models / request validators: none defined. No request/response schema validation at the API Gateway layer.
- Binary media types: none defined on the API. Note the creator_lambda still handles `isBase64Encoded` bodies in code (line 60-61).
- CORS settings: none (`Auth` has no `Cors` block). No CORS at all, which is consistent with a private, server-to-server API.
- Gateway responses: none customized.

### 2. `ReporterFunction`, `GetErasureStatus`, and other API-attached functions

`ReporterFunction` (lines 233-262):

```yaml
ReporterFunction:
  Type: AWS::Serverless::Function
  ...
    Handler: dns_de_rte_orchestrator.reporter_lambda.lambda_handler
    Runtime: python3.13
    Environment:
      Variables:
        DYNAMO_DB_AUDIT_LOGGING_TABLE: !Ref AuditLogDynamoDBTable
        AUDIT_LOG_S3_BUCKET: !Ref AuditLogS3Bucket
        AUDIT_LOG_S3_PREFIX: !Ref AuditLogS3Prefix
        POWERTOOLS_SERVICE_NAME: !Sub "status-erasure-request-${Environment}"
    Events:
      GetErasureStatus:
        Type: Api
        Properties:
          RestApiId: !Ref CreateRequestApi
          Path: /status/{id}
          Method: get
          Auth:
            ApiKeyRequired: true
```

Other functions on the SAME API (`CreateRequestApi`):
- `CreateFunction` (lines 77-112), handler `creator_lambda.lambda_handler`, has event `CreateErasureRequest`: `Path: /erasure-request`, `Method: post`, `Auth: ApiKeyRequired: true`.

Functions NOT attached to the API:
- `ProcessFunction` (lines 162-192), handler `processor_lambda.lambda_handler` — has no `Events` block, so it is not invoked via API Gateway. Its handler ignores the event (`_event`) and runs batch processing driven by remaining-time budget (`context.get_remaining_time_in_millis()`), so it is triggered out-of-band (manual invoke or, presumably, a schedule configured elsewhere — none present in this template).

So the API exposes exactly two routes: `POST /erasure-request` (create) and `GET /status/{id}` (reporter/status).

### 3. API key auth — usage plan, key resource, and how the key is passed

There IS a complete API key setup:

- `CreateRequestApiKey` (lines 114-121): `AWS::ApiGateway::ApiKey`, named `rte-orchestrator-api-key-${Environment}`, `Enabled: true`, depends on the auto-generated `CreateRequestApiStage`. Described as "API key for OneTrust to invoke the RTE create-request API".
- `CreateRequestUsagePlan` (lines 123-132): `AWS::ApiGateway::UsagePlan` tied to `CreateRequestApi` + stage `Environment`. No throttle or quota limits are set on the plan.
- `CreateRequestUsagePlanKey` (lines 134-139): `AWS::ApiGateway::UsagePlanKey` binding the API key to the usage plan (this binding is what actually makes the key valid for the API).

How the key is passed:
- API Gateway REST APIs with `ApiKeyRequired: true` expect the key in the HTTP header `x-api-key`. There is no custom `ApiKeySourceType` override, so it defaults to HEADER (the `x-api-key` header), not a query string.
- The key VALUE is not in the template — Output `CreateRequestApiKeyId` (lines 268-270) only exposes the key ID and notes "retrieve value from API Gateway console".

Would a browser-based GET to `/status/{id}` work with `ApiKeyRequired: true`? No, not as a plain browser navigation, for two independent reasons:
1. The API is PRIVATE and locked to the VPC endpoint via the resource policy (`aws:SourceVpce` condition). A browser outside that VPC cannot reach it at all — it would get a 403 from the resource policy.
2. Even from within the VPC, a normal browser GET (typing the URL) cannot set the required `x-api-key` header, so API Gateway returns 403 `Forbidden` / missing API key. There is also no CORS configuration, so a cross-origin `fetch` from a web page would be blocked. The endpoint is designed for programmatic server-to-server calls (e.g., OneTrust) that explicitly send `x-api-key`.

### 4. Other Lambda handlers in `src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/` and response formatting

Three handler modules exist (all `lambda_handler` entrypoints):

- `reporter_lambda.py` (known to you) — uses a private `_response(status_code, body)` helper (lines 22-30) returning `{"statusCode", "headers": {"Content-Type": "application/json"}, "body": json.dumps(body)}`.
- `creator_lambda.py` — uses an identical `_response` helper (lines 124-132), same shape. Body typed as `dict[str, str | int]`. Returns 200/400/409/500. Handles base64-encoded bodies (`isBase64Encoded`).
- `processor_lambda.py` — NOT an API handler. Its `lambda_handler` returns `None` (line 95) and does no HTTP-style response formatting; it ignores the event payload and runs batch processing.

The two API-facing handlers each define their own local `_response` JSON helper rather than sharing one. The helpers are byte-for-byte equivalent in structure (the only difference is the body type annotation), so there is some duplication if you plan to add a new API handler — you may want to factor a shared response helper. There is no shared HTTP/JSON utility module currently. Other supporting modules (`audit_logging.py`, `request_repository.py`, `request_processor.py`, `customer_api_client.py`, `erasure_request.py`, `as400.py`, `onetrust.py`, `dryrun.py`) are library/service code, not Lambda entrypoints.

- `CreateRequestUsagePlanKey` (lines 134-139): `AWS::ApiGateway::UsagePlanKey` binding the API key to the usage plan (this binding is what actually makes the key valid for the API).

How the key is passed:
- API Gateway REST APIs with `ApiKeyRequired: true` expect the key in the HTTP header `x-api-key`. There is no custom `ApiKeySourceType` override, so it defaults to HEADER (the `x-api-key` header), not a query string.
- The key VALUE is not in the template — Output `CreateRequestApiKeyId` (lines 268-270) only exposes the key ID and notes "retrieve value from API Gateway console".

Would a browser-based GET to `/status/{id}` work with `ApiKeyRequired: true`? No, not as a plain browser navigation, for two independent reasons:
1. The API is PRIVATE and locked to the VPC endpoint via the resource policy (`aws:SourceVpce` condition). A browser outside that VPC cannot reach it at all — it would get a 403 from the resource policy.
2. Even from within the VPC, a normal browser GET (typing the URL) cannot set the required `x-api-key` header, so API Gateway returns 403 `Forbidden` / missing API key. There is also no CORS configuration, so a cross-origin `fetch` from a web page would be blocked. The endpoint is designed for programmatic server-to-server calls (e.g., OneTrust) that explicitly send `x-api-key`.

### 4. Other Lambda handlers in `src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/` and response formatting

Three handler modules exist (all `lambda_handler` entrypoints):

- `reporter_lambda.py` (known to you) — uses a private `_response(status_code, body)` helper (lines 22-30) returning `{"statusCode", "headers": {"Content-Type": "application/json"}, "body": json.dumps(body)}`.
- `creator_lambda.py` — uses an identical `_response` helper (lines 124-132), same shape. Body typed as `dict[str, str | int]`. Returns 200/400/409/500. Handles base64-encoded bodies (`isBase64Encoded`).
- `processor_lambda.py` — NOT an API handler. Its `lambda_handler` returns `None` (line 95) and does no HTTP-style response formatting; it ignores the event payload and runs batch processing.

The two API-facing handlers each define their own local `_response` JSON helper rather than sharing one. The helpers are byte-for-byte equivalent in structure (the only difference is the body type annotation), so there is some duplication if you plan to add a new API handler — you may want to factor a shared response helper. There is no shared HTTP/JSON utility module currently. Other supporting modules (`audit_logging.py`, `request_repository.py`, `request_processor.py`, `customer_api_client.py`, `erasure_request.py`, `as400.py`, `onetrust.py`, `dryrun.py`) are library/service code, not Lambda entrypoints.

# Audit Logging Data Model Report

All code lives in **`/Users/saurabhtyagi/Documents/workspace/dns-de-rte-orchestrator/src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/audit_logging.py`** (435 lines). Note: the class is named **`AuditEntry`**, not `AuditLogEntry`. There is no `AuditLogEntry` in the codebase.

## 1. The `AuditEntry` dataclass (lines 29-54)

A `@dataclass(frozen=True)` — it is immutable. Defined as "A single audit log entry for an erasure request."

**Instance fields (3):**
| Field | Type | Notes |
|-------|------|-------|
| `id` | `str` | Same value as an erasure request's id |
| `timestamp` | `datetime` | Always UTC (coerced on write) |
| `message` | `str` | Free text; avoid raw personal info |

**Class-level field-name constants (`ClassVar[Literal[...]]`):**
- `ID_FIELD = "id"`
- `TIMESTAMP_FIELD = "timestamp"`
- `MESSAGE_FIELD = "message"`

**`to_json()` -> `dict[str, str]`** (lines 41-46). Exact returned shape:
```python
{
    "id": self.id,                      # str
    "timestamp": self.timestamp.isoformat(),  # ISO-8601 string, e.g. "2026-05-29T12:34:56.789000+00:00"
    "message": self.message,            # str
}
```
Important: in `to_json()` the timestamp is serialized as an **ISO-8601 string** via `datetime.isoformat()`. All three values are strings.

**`from_json(cls, data) -> Self`** (lines 48-54) is the inverse — reads the same three keys and parses `timestamp` with `datetime.fromisoformat(...)`.

## 2. `FallbackAuditLogReader` and `get_audit_log_reader()`

### `FallbackAuditLogReader` (lines 284-298)
```python
def __init__(self, primary: AuditLogger, fallback: S3AuditLogReader) -> None:
    self.primary: AuditLogger = primary
    self.fallback: S3AuditLogReader = fallback

def get_logs(self, id: str) -> list[AuditEntry]:
    primary_results = self.primary.get_logs(id)
    if primary_results:
        return primary_results
    logger.info("No audit logs found in primary store for id %s, falling back to S3", id)
    return self.fallback.get_logs(id)
```
- `get_logs(erasure_id)` returns **`list[AuditEntry]`** (a list of `AuditEntry` objects, NOT JSON dicts — the reporter lambda converts them with `entry.to_json()` afterward).
- **Fallback logic:** queries the primary first (DynamoDB). If the primary returns a **non-empty** list, that is returned. Only if the primary list is **empty/falsy** does it fall back to the S3 reader. The fallback is by emptiness, not by exception — a primary exception propagates.
- Note the parameter is named `id`, not `erasure_id`, in the source (the caller `reporter_lambda.py` passes `erasure_id` positionally).

### `get_audit_log_reader() -> FallbackAuditLogReader` (lines 395-409)
- **primary** = `get_audit_logger()`, which returns a `DynamoDBAuditLogger` when env var `DYNAMO_DB_AUDIT_LOGGING_TABLE` is set, otherwise a `NoOpAuditLogger` (whose `get_logs` always returns `[]`, forcing fallback to S3).
- **fallback** = `get_s3_audit_log_reader(bucket, prefix)`.
- Reads env vars `AUDIT_LOG_S3_BUCKET` (required — raises `ValueError` if missing/empty) and `AUDIT_LOG_S3_PREFIX` (optional, defaults to `""`).

### The two underlying sources

**DynamoDB (`DynamoDBAuditLogger.get_logs`, lines 147-163):** paginated `query` on `#id = :id` with `ScanIndexForward=True` (chronological). DynamoDB stores the timestamp differently from the JSON form — see the storage detail below.

**S3 (`S3AuditLogReader.get_logs`, lines 261-281):** reads one gzipped JSON Lines object per erasure id at key `{prefix}/{id}.jsonl.gz` (or `{id}.jsonl.gz` if no prefix — see `_s3_key_for`, lines 239-243). Decompresses with gzip, splits into lines, and parses each line via `AuditEntry.from_json(json.loads(line))`. Returns `[]` (no exception) when the object/bucket is missing (`NoSuchKey`, `404`, `NoSuchBucket`); other `ClientError`s re-raise.

The write side (`S3AuditLogWriter`, lines 301-326) sorts entries by timestamp ascending, joins `json.dumps(entry.to_json())` with newlines, gzips, and `put_object`s — guaranteeing chronological order on disk. `AuditArchiver` (lines 329-349) writes to S3 first, then deletes from DynamoDB, so the fallback never returns empty mid-archive.

## 3. Timestamp formats, states, and constants

### Two distinct timestamp representations (important)
- **JSON / S3 form** (`to_json`): ISO-8601 string via `datetime.isoformat()`.
- **DynamoDB form** (`_create_item`, lines 210-218): timestamp stored as **epoch milliseconds** in a Numeric attribute — `{"N": str(int(timestamp.timestamp() * 1000))}`. On read (`_item_to_entry`, lines 220-236) it divides by 1000 and rebuilds a UTC `datetime`. So the same logical `AuditEntry` is `"N"` ms in Dynamo but an ISO string in S3/JSON.

DynamoDB item shape (`_create_item`):
```python
{
    "id":        {"S": id},
    "timestamp": {"N": str(timestamp_ms)},   # epoch milliseconds
    "message":   {"S": message},
}
```

All timestamps are coerced to **UTC** on write (`datetime.now(timezone.utc)` if none given, else `timestamp.replace(tzinfo=timezone.utc)`) in both `InMemoryAuditLogger.log` and `DynamoDBAuditLogger.log`.

### No status/action enums inside audit logging
The audit module has **no request-state or action-type enums**. `message` is arbitrary free text. There are no `Enum`/`StrEnum`/`IntEnum` classes anywhere in the package.

### Module-level constants in audit_logging.py (lines 352-372)
- `DYNAMO_BATCH_WRITE_MAX_ITEMS: Final[int] = 25` (AWS BatchWriteItem hard limit)
- `DYNAMO_BATCH_WRITE_MAX_ATTEMPTS: Final[int] = 5`
- `BACKOFF_BASE_SECONDS: Final[float] = 0.1`, `BACKOFF_MAX_SECONDS: Final[float] = 2.0` (used by `_backoff_seconds`, capped exponential backoff for batch-delete retries)
- Env-var name constants (`Literal`): `DYNAMO_DB_AUDIT_LOGGING_TABLE`, `AUDIT_LOG_S3_BUCKET`, `AUDIT_LOG_S3_PREFIX`

### Related state enum (direct dependency — `erasure_request.py`)
The `id` on an `AuditEntry` corresponds to an `ErasureRequest.id`. Erasure request states are defined in **`/Users/saurabhtyagi/Documents/workspace/dns-de-rte-orchestrator/src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/erasure_request.py`** (lines 5-37) as a `Literal` type plus a tuple, not an Enum:
```python
type ErasureState = Literal[
    "new", "in-progress", "phase-1", "phase-2", "phase-3", "done", "need-more-detail"
]
VALID_STATES: tuple[ErasureState, ...] = (
    "new", "in-progress", "phase-1", "phase-2", "phase-3", "done", "need-more-detail",
)
```
These states are not stored on `AuditEntry` directly — they live on `ErasureRequest` and would only appear inside an audit `message` if a caller chose to include them.

## Reporter Lambda response shape (consumer context)
`/Users/saurabhtyagi/Documents/workspace/dns-de-rte-orchestrator/src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/reporter_lambda.py` is the only consumer of `get_audit_log_reader()`. On success (200) it returns:
```json
{
  "statusCode": 200,
  "headers": {"Content-Type": "application/json"},
  "body": "{\"id\": \"<erasure_id>\", \"entries\": [ {\"id\":..., \"timestamp\":..., \"message\":...}, ... ]}"
}
```
Each element of `entries` is exactly the `AuditEntry.to_json()` shape. It returns 400 (missing id), 404 (`reader.get_logs` returns empty -> `{"error": "no audit log found", "id": ...}`), or 500 on exception.

### Other files
The matches under `.aws-sam/build/...` and `.claude/worktrees/...` are build artifacts / worktree copies of the same module, not separate sources.

content.text:
I have everything needed. This was a read-only exploration request, so there is no implementation plan to execute. Here is my report.

## Testing Conventions Report: dns-de-rte-orchestrator

### 1. Reporter Lambda and Audit Logging Tests

**Reporter lambda tests** — `/Users/saurabhtyagi/Documents/workspace/dns-de-rte-orchestrator/tests/test_reporter_lambda.py`

Key structural patterns:

- **Env vars set at module import time** (lines 18-28) *before* importing `reporter_lambda`, because the module runs `reader = get_audit_log_reader()` at import time (reporter_lambda.py line 19). Uses `os.environ.setdefault(...)` for DynamoDB table, S3 bucket/prefix, repository type, and fake AWS credentials/region. Imports are deferred with `# noqa: E402`.
- **No moto here** — reporter tests inject a hand-written stub `_RecordingReader` (lines 68-77) that records `get_logs` calls and either returns a `list[AuditEntry]` or raises a queued `Exception`. The stub is swapped in via `monkeypatch.setattr(reporter_module, "reader", _RecordingReader(...))`.
- **Building fake AuditLogEntry objects**: They use the real `AuditEntry` dataclass directly, e.g. `AuditEntry(id="req-123", timestamp=TS, message="created")` (lines 106-113). `TS` is a fixed `datetime(2026, 5, 7, 14, 30, 45, tzinfo=timezone.utc)` (line 97).
- **Invoking lambda_handler**: helpers `make_get_event(id)` (lines 80-84), `make_context()` returning `cast(LambdaContext, MagicMock())` (lines 87-88), and `invoke(event)` calling `lambda_handler(event, make_context())` with a `cast` because powertools decorators erase the return type (lines 91-93).
- **Response parsing**: typed helpers `_parse_ok`, `_parse_not_found`, `_parse_error` (lines 50-65) using `TypedDict` shapes and `json.loads(response["body"])`.
- **Fixture loading**: `FIXTURES_DIR = Path(__file__).parent / "fixtures"` and `load_fixture(name)` (lines 96-102), driven via `@mark.parametrize`.

**Audit logging tests** — two files:

`/Users/saurabhtyagi/Documents/workspace/dns-de-rte-orchestrator/tests/test_audit_logging.py` (unit tests, no real AWS):
- Hand-written stubs `StubDynamoDBClient` and `StubDynamoDBPaginator` (lines 41-84) capturing `put_item`/`batch_write_item` calls and returning queued query pages.
- S3 client stubbed with `MagicMock` via `_make_stub_s3(...)` and `_make_stub_s3_writer()` (lines 368-387, 830-833), using `ClientError` side effects to simulate `NoSuchKey`/`AccessDenied`.
- `boto3.client` and factory functions monkeypatched by fully-qualified path, e.g. `monkeypatch.setattr("dns_de_rte_orchestrator.audit_logging.boto3.client", fake_boto3_client)`.
- `time.sleep` monkeypatched for retry tests (lines 760, 800).
- Builds `AuditEntry(id=..., timestamp=..., message=...)` throughout.

`/Users/saurabhtyagi/Documents/workspace/dns-de-rte-orchestrator/tests/test_dynamodb_audit_logger.py` (integration-style with moto):
- `@pytest.fixture` `dynamodb_table` using `with mock_aws():` to create a real moto DynamoDB table (lines 26-52). Table key schema uses `AuditEntry.ID_FIELD` (HASH) and `AuditEntry.TIMESTAMP_FIELD` (RANGE), `BillingMode="PAY_PER_REQUEST"`, region `eu-west-1`.
- `build_audit_logger()` constructs a real boto3-backed `DynamoDBAuditLogger` (lines 55-60).

**Two AWS-mocking strategies coexist**: lightweight stubs/MagicMock for unit tests, and `moto`'s `mock_aws` for integration tests. Files using moto: `test_dynamodb_audit_logger.py`, `test_onetrust_secrets.py`, `test_dynamodb_request_repository.py`, `test_s3_audit_log_writer.py`, `test_s3_audit_log_reader.py`. There is no `tests/conftest.py` (the search found none; fixtures are defined per-file).

The `AuditEntry` source is at `/Users/saurabhtyagi/Documents/workspace/dns-de-rte-orchestrator/src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/audit_logging.py` (lines 29-54): a `@dataclass(frozen=True)` with `id: str`, `timestamp: datetime`, `message: str`, plus `to_json()` / `from_json()` and `ClassVar` field-name constants.

### 2. API Gateway Proxy Event Mocking

Two approaches:

- **Inline minimal dicts** in `test_reporter_lambda.py`: `make_get_event` returns `{"pathParameters": {"id": id} if id is not None else None, "httpMethod": "GET"}` (lines 80-84). Edge cases tested: `pathParameters` set to `None`, key entirely absent (`{}`), and whitespace-only id. The lambda reads via `event.get("pathParameters")`, guards with `isinstance(raw_params, dict)`, then `path_parameters.get("id")` (reporter_lambda.py lines 40-47).
- **Full JSON fixtures** in `/Users/saurabhtyagi/Documents/workspace/dns-de-rte-orchestrator/tests/fixtures/` (e.g. `reporter_get_status_s3_and_dynamodb.json`). These model a complete API Gateway proxy event: `httpMethod`, `path`, `pathParameters: {"id": "ERASURE-7384"}`, `queryStringParameters`, `headers` (incl. `x-api-key`), `requestContext` with `resourcePath: "/status/{id}"`, `stage`, `requestId`, `identity.sourceIp`, `body`, `isBase64Encoded`. Available fixtures: `reporter_get_status.json`, `reporter_get_status_s3_and_dynamodb.json`, `reporter_get_status_dynamodb_only.json`, `reporter_get_status_s3_only.json`, `reporter_get_status_unknown_id.json`, `reporter_missing_id.json`, `reporter_audit_log_cases.json`.

### 3. HTML Rendering / Templating

**No jinja2 anywhere** — zero matches in `.py`, `.toml`, or lockfiles. **jinja2 is NOT a dependency** in `pyproject.toml` (neither in `dependencies` nor the `dev` group), and no HTML templates (`.html`/`.j2`) exist in the repo.

### 4. Dependency Setup (pyproject.toml)

`/Users/saurabhtyagi/Documents/workspace/dns-de-rte-orchestrator/pyproject.toml`:

I have everything needed. This was a read-only exploration request, so there is no implementation plan to execute. Here is my report.

## Testing Conventions Report: dns-de-rte-orchestrator

### 1. Reporter Lambda and Audit Logging Tests

**Reporter lambda tests** — `/Users/saurabhtyagi/Documents/workspace/dns-de-rte-orchestrator/tests/test_reporter_lambda.py`

Key structural patterns:

- **Env vars set at module import time** (lines 18-28) *before* importing `reporter_lambda`, because the module runs `reader = get_audit_log_reader()` at import time (reporter_lambda.py line 19). Uses `os.environ.setdefault(...)` for DynamoDB table, S3 bucket/prefix, repository type, and fake AWS credentials/region. Imports are deferred with `# noqa: E402`.
- **No moto here** — reporter tests inject a hand-written stub `_RecordingReader` (lines 68-77) that records `get_logs` calls and either returns a `list[AuditEntry]` or raises a queued `Exception`. The stub is swapped in via `monkeypatch.setattr(reporter_module, "reader", _RecordingReader(...))`.
- **Building fake AuditLogEntry objects**: They use the real `AuditEntry` dataclass directly, e.g. `AuditEntry(id="req-123", timestamp=TS, message="created")` (lines 106-113). `TS` is a fixed `datetime(2026, 5, 7, 14, 30, 45, tzinfo=timezone.utc)` (line 97).
- **Invoking lambda_handler**: helpers `make_get_event(id)` (lines 80-84), `make_context()` returning `cast(LambdaContext, MagicMock())` (lines 87-88), and `invoke(event)` calling `lambda_handler(event, make_context())` with a `cast` because powertools decorators erase the return type (lines 91-93).
- **Response parsing**: typed helpers `_parse_ok`, `_parse_not_found`, `_parse_error` (lines 50-65) using `TypedDict` shapes and `json.loads(response["body"])`.
- **Fixture loading**: `FIXTURES_DIR = Path(__file__).parent / "fixtures"` and `load_fixture(name)` (lines 96-102), driven via `@mark.parametrize`.

**Audit logging tests** — two files:

`/Users/saurabhtyagi/Documents/workspace/dns-de-rte-orchestrator/tests/test_audit_logging.py` (unit tests, no real AWS):
- Hand-written stubs `StubDynamoDBClient` and `StubDynamoDBPaginator` (lines 41-84) capturing `put_item`/`batch_write_item` calls and returning queued query pages.
- S3 client stubbed with `MagicMock` via `_make_stub_s3(...)` and `_make_stub_s3_writer()` (lines 368-387, 830-833), using `ClientError` side effects to simulate `NoSuchKey`/`AccessDenied`.
- `boto3.client` and factory functions monkeypatched by fully-qualified path, e.g. `monkeypatch.setattr("dns_de_rte_orchestrator.audit_logging.boto3.client", fake_boto3_client)`.
- `time.sleep` monkeypatched for retry tests (lines 760, 800).
- Builds `AuditEntry(id=..., timestamp=..., message=...)` throughout.

`/Users/saurabhtyagi/Documents/workspace/dns-de-rte-orchestrator/tests/test_dynamodb_audit_logger.py` (integration-style with moto):
- `@pytest.fixture` `dynamodb_table` using `with mock_aws():` to create a real moto DynamoDB table (lines 26-52). Table key schema uses `AuditEntry.ID_FIELD` (HASH) and `AuditEntry.TIMESTAMP_FIELD` (RANGE), `BillingMode="PAY_PER_REQUEST"`, region `eu-west-1`.
- `build_audit_logger()` constructs a real boto3-backed `DynamoDBAuditLogger` (lines 55-60).

**Two AWS-mocking strategies coexist**: lightweight stubs/MagicMock for unit tests, and `moto`'s `mock_aws` for integration tests. Files using moto: `test_dynamodb_audit_logger.py`, `test_onetrust_secrets.py`, `test_dynamodb_request_repository.py`, `test_s3_audit_log_writer.py`, `test_s3_audit_log_reader.py`. There is no `tests/conftest.py` (the search found none; fixtures are defined per-file).

The `AuditEntry` source is at `/Users/saurabhtyagi/Documents/workspace/dns-de-rte-orchestrator/src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/audit_logging.py` (lines 29-54): a `@dataclass(frozen=True)` with `id: str`, `timestamp: datetime`, `message: str`, plus `to_json()` / `from_json()` and `ClassVar` field-name constants.

### 2. API Gateway Proxy Event Mocking

Two approaches:

- **Inline minimal dicts** in `test_reporter_lambda.py`: `make_get_event` returns `{"pathParameters": {"id": id} if id is not None else None, "httpMethod": "GET"}` (lines 80-84). Edge cases tested: `pathParameters` set to `None`, key entirely absent (`{}`), and whitespace-only id. The lambda reads via `event.get("pathParameters")`, guards with `isinstance(raw_params, dict)`, then `path_parameters.get("id")` (reporter_lambda.py lines 40-47).
- **Full JSON fixtures** in `/Users/saurabhtyagi/Documents/workspace/dns-de-rte-orchestrator/tests/fixtures/` (e.g. `reporter_get_status_s3_and_dynamodb.json`). These model a complete API Gateway proxy event: `httpMethod`, `path`, `pathParameters: {"id": "ERASURE-7384"}`, `queryStringParameters`, `headers` (incl. `x-api-key`), `requestContext` with `resourcePath: "/status/{id}"`, `stage`, `requestId`, `identity.sourceIp`, `body`, `isBase64Encoded`. Available fixtures: `reporter_get_status.json`, `reporter_get_status_s3_and_dynamodb.json`, `reporter_get_status_dynamodb_only.json`, `reporter_get_status_s3_only.json`, `reporter_get_status_unknown_id.json`, `reporter_missing_id.json`, `reporter_audit_log_cases.json`.

### 3. HTML Rendering / Templating

**No jinja2 anywhere** — zero matches in `.py`, `.toml`, or lockfiles. **jinja2 is NOT a dependency** in `pyproject.toml` (neither in `dependencies` nor the `dev` group), and no HTML templates (`.html`/`.j2`) exist in the repo.

The only "template/render" references are in `/Users/saurabhtyagi/Documents/workspace/dns-de-rte-orchestrator/src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/as400.py`, which uses Python's stdlib `string.Template` (line 6: `from string import Template`) to produce JSON webhook bodies via `template.substitute(...)` (line 89, 118) — not HTML and not a templating library. So a new HTML-rendering feature would need to either add a templating dependency or use stdlib string formatting.

### 4. Dependency Setup (pyproject.toml)

`/Users/saurabhtyagi/Documents/workspace/dns-de-rte-orchestrator/pyproject.toml`:

Runtime dependencies (`requires-python = ">=3.13"`):
- `aws-lambda-powertools[tracer]>=3.28.0` (provides `Logger`, `Tracer`, `LambdaContext` typing — used by reporter_lambda)
- `boto3[crt]==1.43.7`
- `python-dotenv>=1.2.2` (reporter_lambda calls `load_dotenv()`)
- `requests>=2.33.1`

Dev group (`[dependency-groups].dev`):
- `basedpyright>=1.39.2`, `black>=26.3.1`, `debugpy>=1.8.20`
- `moto[dynamodb,s3]>=5.1.22` (AWS mocking)
- `prek>=0.3.9`, `pytest>=9.0.3`, `pytest-cov>=7.1.0`, `pytest-httpserver>=1.1.5`
- `ruff>=0.15.11`, `types-boto3-custom` (vendored wheel via `[tool.uv.sources]`)

Build backend is `uv_build`. Pytest config (`[tool.pytest]`): `testpaths = ["tests"]`, `pythonpath = ["src"]`, `addopts = ["-ra", "-q"]`, `minversion = "9.0"`. Black/ruff line-length 88, target py313. Note tests use `basedpyright`-style strict typing with heavy `cast(...)` usage and `# pyright: ignore[...]` comments — a new test should follow that typed style.

### Recommendations for a new HTML-rendering feature following conventions
- A new reporter-style lambda should set required env vars at import time before importing the module, expose a module-level dependency (like `reader`) that tests can `monkeypatch.setattr`, and return the `{"statusCode", "headers", "body"}` dict shape.
- For HTML, you'd be adding the first templating dependency — either add `jinja2` to `dependencies`, or follow the existing stdlib precedent (`string.Template`) used in `as400.py`.
- Tests: build real `AuditEntry` objects, inject a `_RecordingReader`-style stub or `MagicMock`, mock the API Gateway event either inline (`make_*_event`) or via a JSON fixture under `tests/fixtures/`, and use `cast(LambdaContext, MagicMock())` for context.

I have a thorough understanding now. Key findings before I design:

- **Data**: `AuditEntry` is `{id, timestamp (UTC datetime), message (free text)}`; `reader.get_logs(id)` returns `list[AuditEntry]`. Free-text `message` means HTML output **must** be escaped (XSS).
- **API is PRIVATE**: `CreateRequestApi` is VPC-endpoint-locked via resource policy and **not reachable from the public internet**.
- **API key blocks browsers**: `ApiKeyRequired: true` expects an `x-api-key` *header*. A plain browser navigation can't send that → 403. API Gateway also can't read API keys from the query string.
- **No CORS, no templating lib**: jinja2 is not a dependency; the repo's only templating precedent is stdlib `string.Template` (`as400.py`).

These surface two real design decisions (access/auth and where the HTML lives) that change the plan, so let me confirm them with you before finalizing.

questions.question: The API is PRIVATE (VPC-endpoint-locked) and requires an x-api-key header, which a browser can't send on plain navigation. How should the browser HTML page authenticate / be reached?

options.label: No key, rely on VPC
options.description: Add the HTML route with ApiKeyRequired:false, relying on the existing PRIVATE VPC-endpoint resource policy for security. Internal users on the corporate network/VPC navigate directly. Recommended — the API key was added for OneTrust's write endpoint, not internal reads.

options.label: Keep API key required
options.description: Browser users would need a browser extension or tool to inject the x-api-key header; plain URL navigation will not work. Most secure but poor UX for a browser page.

options.label: Public internet access
options.description: Make the API publicly reachable (drop PRIVATE endpoint type, add WAF/throttling). Large security-sensitive change beyond this feature.

questions.question: Where should the HTML rendering live relative to the existing JSON endpoint?

options.label: New /status/{id}/view route
options.description: Add a second API event to the existing ReporterFunction returning HTML, leaving GET /status/{id} (JSON, api-key) completely untouched for OneTrust/programmatic callers. Cleanest separation; allows different auth per route. Recommended.

options.label: Content-negotiate on /status/{id}
options.description: Same route returns HTML or JSON based on the Accept header / ?format=html. Reuses one route but auth must be shared, so it would change the existing endpoint's auth.

questions.question: How should the HTML be generated?

options.label: Stdlib (no new dep)
options.description: Use html.escape() + stdlib string.Template / f-strings, matching the as400.py precedent. No new dependency, simpler Makefile packaging. Recommended for a single table page.

options.label: Add jinja2
options.description: Add jinja2 as a runtime dependency for richer templating. More power but first templating dep + packaging change.

input.content:
# Plan: Browser-friendly HTML audit status page

## Context

Today the only way to read an erasure request's audit log is `GET /status/{id}` on
`CreateRequestApi`, which returns JSON and requires an `x-api-key` header — designed
for programmatic callers (OneTrust). Internal users want to open a URL in a browser
(on the corporate VPC) and see the audit history rendered as a readable HTML page,
without injecting headers or parsing JSON.

This adds a second, browser-friendly route that renders the same `AuditEntry` data as
an HTML table. It reuses the existing reader and Lambda; only a new event, a handler
branch, a Jinja2 template, and the `jinja2` dependency are added. The existing JSON
endpoint stays byte-for-byte unchanged for OneTrust/programmatic callers.

Decisions confirmed with the user:
- **Auth**: new route uses `ApiKeyRequired: false`, relying on the existing PRIVATE
  VPC-endpoint resource policy (no header needed → works in a browser).
- **Shape**: new route `GET /status/{id}/view` on the **same** `ReporterFunction`
  (second API event); JSON `/status/{id}` left untouched.
- **Templating**: add `jinja2` as a runtime dependency.

## Data shape (from exploration)

- `reader.get_logs(id)` → `list[AuditEntry]`; `AuditEntry` is `@dataclass(frozen=True)`
  with `id: str`, `timestamp: datetime` (UTC), `message: str`
  (`src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/audit_logging.py:29-54`).
- `message` is free text → **must be HTML-escaped** (Jinja2 autoescape handles this).

## Changes

### 1. Add dependency
- `uv add jinja2` → adds to `[project.dependencies]` in `pyproject.toml`.
- No `Makefile` change: `build-ReporterFunction` already runs
  `uv export --no-dev` (picks up new runtime dep) and copies the whole
  `src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator` package (so a template placed inside it is bundled).

### 2. HTML template
- New file `src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/templates/audit_status.html.j2`.
- Renders: page title with erasure id, a table of entries
  (`timestamp` formatted human-readably, `message`), and a friendly empty/error state.
- Jinja2 `Environment(autoescape=True)` so `message` is escaped.

### 3. Reporter Lambda — add HTML branch
File: `src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/reporter_lambda.py`
- Keep the module-level `reader` and existing `_response` (JSON) helper.
- Add a module-level Jinja2 `Environment` using a `PackageLoader`/`FileSystemLoader`
  rooted at the `templates/` dir (resolve via `importlib.resources` / `Path(__file__).parent`),
  built once at import time (mirrors how `reader` is initialised once).
- Extract shared logic into a helper `_load_entries(event) -> tuple[str | None, list[AuditEntry] | None, dict | None]`
  (or simpler: a function returning `(erasure_id, entries)` and raising/encoding the
  error cases) so both routes parse `pathParameters.id` and call `reader.get_logs`
  identically.
- Add `_html_response(status_code, html)` returning
  `{"statusCode", "headers": {"Content-Type": "text/html; charset=utf-8"}, "body": html}`.
- In `lambda_handler`, detect the HTML route via
  `event["requestContext"]["resourcePath"]` (or path `.endswith("/view")`):
  - HTML route: render template for 200 / empty (404-style friendly page) / 400 / 500.
  - Otherwise: existing JSON behaviour, unchanged.
- All HTML error/empty cases render a small friendly page rather than raw JSON.

### 4. SAM template — new event + route
File: `template.yaml` (ReporterFunction, ~lines 233-262)
- Add a second `Events` entry on `ReporterFunction`:
  ```yaml
  GetErasureStatusView:
    Type: Api
    Properties:
      RestApiId: !Ref CreateRequestApi
      Path: /status/{id}/view
      Method: get
      Auth:
        ApiKeyRequired: false
  ```
- No new function, no binary media types (HTML is text), no CORS (single private API,
  same-origin navigation).
- **Invoke the `sam-template` skill before editing `template.yaml`** (project rule).

## Files touched
- `pyproject.toml` (+`jinja2`) and `uv.lock`
- `src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/templates/audit_status.html.j2` (new)
- `src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/reporter_lambda.py`
- `template.yaml`
- `tests/test_reporter_lambda.py` (+ optional fixture)
- `TODO.md` (mark item done — project rule)

## Tests
Follow `tests/test_reporter_lambda.py` conventions (set env vars before import,
`monkeypatch.setattr(reporter_module, "reader", _RecordingReader(...))`, build real
`AuditEntry` objects, `make_*_event` helpers, `cast(LambdaContext, MagicMock())`):
- `make_view_event(id)` with `requestContext.resourcePath = "/status/{id}/view"`.
- 200: response is `text/html`, body contains the rendered timestamp + message rows.
- **XSS**: a `message` like `<script>alert(1)</script>` appears **escaped** in the body.
- Empty entries → friendly HTML "no audit log" page (not JSON).
- Missing/blank id → HTML 400 page.
- Reader raises → HTML 500 page.
- Assert the JSON route `/status/{id}` is unchanged (existing tests still pass).

## Verification
1. `uv sync --all-groups`
2. `uv run pytest` — all tests green, including new HTML cases.

toolUseResult.content:
# Plan: Browser-friendly HTML audit status page

## Context

Today the only way to read an erasure request's audit log is `GET /status/{id}` on
`CreateRequestApi`, which returns JSON and requires an `x-api-key` header — designed
for programmatic callers (OneTrust). Internal users want to open a URL in a browser
(on the corporate VPC) and see the audit history rendered as a readable HTML page,
without injecting headers or parsing JSON.

This adds a second, browser-friendly route that renders the same `AuditEntry` data as
an HTML table. It reuses the existing reader and Lambda; only a new event, a handler
branch, a Jinja2 template, and the `jinja2` dependency are added. The existing JSON
endpoint stays byte-for-byte unchanged for OneTrust/programmatic callers.

Decisions confirmed with the user:
- **Auth**: new route uses `ApiKeyRequired: false`, relying on the existing PRIVATE
  VPC-endpoint resource policy (no header needed → works in a browser).
- **Shape**: new route `GET /status/{id}/view` on the **same** `ReporterFunction`
  (second API event); JSON `/status/{id}` left untouched.
- **Templating**: add `jinja2` as a runtime dependency.

## Data shape (from exploration)

- `reader.get_logs(id)` → `list[AuditEntry]`; `AuditEntry` is `@dataclass(frozen=True)`
  with `id: str`, `timestamp: datetime` (UTC), `message: str`
  (`src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/audit_logging.py:29-54`).
- `message` is free text → **must be HTML-escaped** (Jinja2 autoescape handles this).

## Changes

### 1. Add dependency
- `uv add jinja2` → adds to `[project.dependencies]` in `pyproject.toml`.
- No `Makefile` change: `build-ReporterFunction` already runs
  `uv export --no-dev` (picks up new runtime dep) and copies the whole
  `src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator` package (so a template placed inside it is bundled).

### 2. HTML template
- New file `src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/templates/audit_status.html.j2`.
- Renders: page title with erasure id, a table of entries
  (`timestamp` formatted human-readably, `message`), and a friendly empty/error state.
- Jinja2 `Environment(autoescape=True)` so `message` is escaped.

### 3. Reporter Lambda — add HTML branch
File: `src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/reporter_lambda.py`
- Keep the module-level `reader` and existing `_response` (JSON) helper.
- Add a module-level Jinja2 `Environment` using a `PackageLoader`/`FileSystemLoader`
  rooted at the `templates/` dir (resolve via `importlib.resources` / `Path(__file__).parent`),
  built once at import time (mirrors how `reader` is initialised once).
- Extract shared logic into a helper `_load_entries(event) -> tuple[str | None, list[AuditEntry] | None, dict | None]`
  (or simpler: a function returning `(erasure_id, entries)` and raising/encoding the
  error cases) so both routes parse `pathParameters.id` and call `reader.get_logs`
  identically.
- Add `_html_response(status_code, html)` returning
  `{"statusCode", "headers": {"Content-Type": "text/html; charset=utf-8"}, "body": html}`.
- In `lambda_handler`, detect the HTML route via
  `event["requestContext"]["resourcePath"]` (or path `.endswith("/view")`):
  - HTML route: render template for 200 / empty (404-style friendly page) / 400 / 500.
  - Otherwise: existing JSON behaviour, unchanged.
- All HTML error/empty cases render a small friendly page rather than raw JSON.

### 4. SAM template — new event + route
File: `template.yaml` (ReporterFunction, ~lines 233-262)
- Add a second `Events` entry on `ReporterFunction`:
  ```yaml
  GetErasureStatusView:
    Type: Api
    Properties:
      RestApiId: !Ref CreateRequestApi
      Path: /status/{id}/view
      Method: get
      Auth:
        ApiKeyRequired: false
  ```
- No new function, no binary media types (HTML is text), no CORS (single private API,
  same-origin navigation).
- **Invoke the `sam-template` skill before editing `template.yaml`** (project rule).

## Files touched
- `pyproject.toml` (+`jinja2`) and `uv.lock`
- `src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/templates/audit_status.html.j2` (new)
- `src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/reporter_lambda.py`
- `template.yaml`
- `tests/test_reporter_lambda.py` (+ optional fixture)
- `TODO.md` (mark item done — project rule)

## Tests
Follow `tests/test_reporter_lambda.py` conventions (set env vars before import,
`monkeypatch.setattr(reporter_module, "reader", _RecordingReader(...))`, build real
`AuditEntry` objects, `make_*_event` helpers, `cast(LambdaContext, MagicMock())`):
- `make_view_event(id)` with `requestContext.resourcePath = "/status/{id}/view"`.
- 200: response is `text/html`, body contains the rendered timestamp + message rows.
- **XSS**: a `message` like `<script>alert(1)</script>` appears **escaped** in the body.
- Empty entries → friendly HTML "no audit log" page (not JSON).
- Missing/blank id → HTML 400 page.
- Reader raises → HTML 500 page.
- Assert the JSON route `/status/{id}` is unchanged (existing tests still pass).

## Verification
1. `uv sync --all-groups`
2. `uv run pytest` — all tests green, including new HTML cases.
3. `uv run basedpyright` (or project type-check) clean — no new `Any`/ignores.
4. `sam validate --lint` then `sam build` succeed (confirms jinja2 + template bundle).
5. Manual (optional, in-VPC): open `/status/<id>/view` in a browser → rendered table.

run sam validate and sam build

# Plan: Browser-friendly HTML audit status page

**Branch:** `DATA_4929_html_audit_log` (created from `master`). Verified master already
contains every dependency this plan needs — `reporter_lambda.py`, `FallbackAuditLogReader`
/ `S3AuditLogReader` / `get_audit_log_reader()` in `audit_logging.py`, and the
`CreateRequestApi` / `ReporterFunction` / API-key resources in `template.yaml`. No
rebase or cherry-pick from the old DATA-4715 branch is required.

## Context

Today the only way to read an erasure request's audit log is `GET /status/{id}` on
`CreateRequestApi`, which returns JSON and requires an `x-api-key` header — designed
for programmatic callers (OneTrust). Internal users want to open a URL in a browser
(on the corporate VPC) and see the audit history rendered as a readable HTML page,
without injecting headers or parsing JSON.

This adds a second, browser-friendly route that renders the same `AuditEntry` data as
an HTML table. It reuses the existing reader and Lambda; only a new event, a handler
branch, a Jinja2 template, and the `jinja2` dependency are added. The existing JSON
endpoint stays byte-for-byte unchanged for OneTrust/programmatic callers.

Decisions confirmed with the user:
- **Auth**: new route uses `ApiKeyRequired: false`, relying on the existing PRIVATE
  VPC-endpoint resource policy (no header needed → works in a browser).
- **Shape**: new route `GET /status/{id}/view` on the **same** `ReporterFunction`
  (second API event); JSON `/status/{id}` left untouched.
- **Templating**: add `jinja2` as a runtime dependency.

## Data shape (from exploration)

- `reader.get_logs(id)` → `list[AuditEntry]`; `AuditEntry` is `@dataclass(frozen=True)`
  with `id: str`, `timestamp: datetime` (UTC), `message: str`
  (`src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/audit_logging.py:29-54`).
- `message` is free text → **must be HTML-escaped** (Jinja2 autoescape handles this).

## Changes

### 1. Add dependency
- `uv add jinja2` → adds to `[project.dependencies]` in `pyproject.toml`.
- No `Makefile` change: `build-ReporterFunction` already runs
  `uv export --no-dev` (picks up new runtime dep) and copies the whole
  `src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator` package (so a template placed inside it is bundled).

### 2. HTML template
- New file `src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/templates/audit_status.html.j2`.
- Renders: page title with erasure id, a table of entries
  (`timestamp` formatted human-readably, `message`), and a friendly empty/error state.
- Jinja2 `Environment(autoescape=True)` so `message` is escaped.

### 3. Reporter Lambda — add HTML branch
File: `src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/reporter_lambda.py`
- Keep the module-level `reader` and existing `_response` (JSON) helper.
- Add a module-level Jinja2 `Environment` using a `PackageLoader`/`FileSystemLoader`
  rooted at the `templates/` dir (resolve via `importlib.resources` / `Path(__file__).parent`),
  built once at import time (mirrors how `reader` is initialised once).
- Extract shared logic into a helper `_load_entries(event) -> tuple[str | None, list[AuditEntry] | None, dict | None]`
  (or simpler: a function returning `(erasure_id, entries)` and raising/encoding the
  error cases) so both routes parse `pathParameters.id` and call `reader.get_logs`
  identically.
- Add `_html_response(status_code, html)` returning
  `{"statusCode", "headers": {"Content-Type": "text/html; charset=utf-8"}, "body": html}`.
- In `lambda_handler`, detect the HTML route via
  `event["requestContext"]["resourcePath"]` (or path `.endswith("/view")`):
  - HTML route: render template for 200 / empty (404-style friendly page) / 400 / 500.
  - Otherwise: existing JSON behaviour, unchanged.
- All HTML error/empty cases render a small friendly page rather than raw JSON.

### 4. SAM template — new event + route
File: `template.yaml` (ReporterFunction, ~lines 233-262)
- Add a second `Events` entry on `ReporterFunction`:
  ```yaml
  GetErasureStatusView:
    Type: Api
    Properties:
      RestApiId: !Ref CreateRequestApi
      Path: /status/{id}/view
      Method: get
      Auth:
        ApiKeyRequired: false
  ```
- No new function, no binary media types (HTML is text), no CORS (single private API,
  same-origin navigation).
- **Invoke the `sam-template` skill before editing `template.yaml`** (project rule).

## Files touched
- `pyproject.toml` (+`jinja2`) and `uv.lock`
- `src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/templates/audit_status.html.j2` (new)
- `src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/reporter_lambda.py`
- `template.yaml`
- `tests/test_reporter_lambda.py` (+ optional fixture)
- `TODO.md` (mark item done — project rule)

## Tests
Follow `tests/test_reporter_lambda.py` conventions (set env vars before import,
`monkeypatch.setattr(reporter_module, "reader", _RecordingReader(...))`, build real
`AuditEntry` objects, `make_*_event` helpers, `cast(LambdaContext, MagicMock())`):
- `make_view_event(id)` with `requestContext.resourcePath = "/status/{id}/view"`.
- 200: response is `text/html`, body contains the rendered timestamp + message rows.
- **XSS**: a `message` like `<script>alert(1)</script>` appears **escaped** in the body.
- Empty entries → friendly HTML "no audit log" page (not JSON).
- Missing/blank id → HTML 400 page.
- Reader raises → HTML 500 page.
- Assert the JSON route `/status/{id}` is unchanged (existing tests still pass).

## Verification
1. `uv sync --all-groups`
2. `uv run pytest` — all tests green, including new HTML cases.
3. `uv run basedpyright` (or project type-check) clean — no new `Any`/ignores.
4. `sam validate --lint` then `sam build` succeed (confirms jinja2 + template bundle).
5. Manual (optional, in-VPC): open `/status/<id>/view` in a browser → rendered table.

## Approved Plan:
# Plan: Browser-friendly HTML audit status page

**Branch:** `DATA_4929_html_audit_log` (created from `master`). Verified master already
contains every dependency this plan needs — `reporter_lambda.py`, `FallbackAuditLogReader`
/ `S3AuditLogReader` / `get_audit_log_reader()` in `audit_logging.py`, and the
`CreateRequestApi` / `ReporterFunction` / API-key resources in `template.yaml`. No
rebase or cherry-pick from the old DATA-4715 branch is required.

## Prerequisite (branch setup)

Work must NOT continue on `DATA_4715_audit_log_to_s3`. Before any change, switch to a
fresh branch cut from `master`:

```sh
git checkout master && git pull --ff-only
git checkout -b DATA_4929_html_audit_log
```

(Done — branch `DATA_4929_html_audit_log` is checked out from up-to-date `master`.)
All edits in this plan land on that branch.

## Context

Today the only way to read an erasure request's audit log is `GET /status/{id}` on
`CreateRequestApi`, which returns JSON and requires an `x-api-key` header — designed
for programmatic callers (OneTrust). Internal users want to open a URL in a browser
(on the corporate VPC) and see the audit history rendered as a readable HTML page,
without injecting headers or parsing JSON.

This adds a second, browser-friendly route that renders the same `AuditEntry` data as
an HTML table. It reuses the existing reader and Lambda; only a new event, a handler
branch, a Jinja2 template, and the `jinja2` dependency are added. The existing JSON
endpoint stays byte-for-byte unchanged for OneTrust/programmatic callers.

Decisions confirmed with the user:
- **Auth**: new route uses `ApiKeyRequired: false`, relying on the existing PRIVATE
  VPC-endpoint resource policy (no header needed → works in a browser).
- **Shape**: new route `GET /status/{id}/view` on the **same** `ReporterFunction`
  (second API event); JSON `/status/{id}` left untouched.
- **Templating**: add `jinja2` as a runtime dependency.

## Data shape (from exploration)

- `reader.get_logs(id)` → `list[AuditEntry]`; `AuditEntry` is `@dataclass(frozen=True)`
  with `id: str`, `timestamp: datetime` (UTC), `message: str`
  (`src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/audit_logging.py:29-54`).
- `message` is free text → **must be HTML-escaped** (Jinja2 autoescape handles this).

## Changes

### 1. Add dependency
- `uv add jinja2` → adds to `[project.dependencies]` in `pyproject.toml`.
- No `Makefile` change: `build-ReporterFunction` already runs
  `uv export --no-dev` (picks up new runtime dep) and copies the whole
  `src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator` package (so a template placed inside it is bundled).

### 2. HTML template
- New file `src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/templates/audit_status.html.j2`.
- Renders: page title with erasure id, a table of entries
  (`timestamp` formatted human-readably, `message`), and a friendly empty/error state.
- Jinja2 `Environment(autoescape=True)` so `message` is escaped.

### 3. Reporter Lambda — add HTML branch
File: `src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/reporter_lambda.py`
- Keep the module-level `reader` and existing `_response` (JSON) helper.
- Add a module-level Jinja2 `Environment` using a `PackageLoader`/`FileSystemLoader`
  rooted at the `templates/` dir (resolve via `importlib.resources` / `Path(__file__).parent`),
  built once at import time (mirrors how `reader` is initialised once).
- Extract shared logic into a helper `_load_entries(event) -> tuple[str | None, list[AuditEntry] | None, dict | None]`
  (or simpler: a function returning `(erasure_id, entries)` and raising/encoding the
  error cases) so both routes parse `pathParameters.id` and call `reader.get_logs`
  identically.
- Add `_html_response(status_code, html)` returning
  `{"statusCode", "headers": {"Content-Type": "text/html; charset=utf-8"}, "body": html}`.
- In `lambda_handler`, detect the HTML route via
  `event["requestContext"]["resourcePath"]` (or path `.endswith("/view")`):
  - HTML route: render template for 200 / empty (404-style friendly page) / 400 / 500.
  - Otherwise: existing JSON behaviour, unchanged.
- All HTML error/empty cases render a small friendly page rather than raw JSON.

### 4. SAM template — new event + route
File: `template.yaml` (ReporterFunction at line 216 on this branch; existing
`GetErasureStatus` event at line 238, `Path: /status/{id}`)
- Add a second `Events` entry on `ReporterFunction`:
  ```yaml
  GetErasureStatusView:
    Type: Api
    Properties:
      RestApiId: !Ref CreateRequestApi
      Path: /status/{id}/view
      Method: get
      Auth:
        ApiKeyRequired: false
  ```
- No new function, no binary media types (HTML is text), no CORS (single private API,
  same-origin navigation).
- **Invoke the `sam-template` skill before editing `template.yaml`** (project rule).

## Files touched
- `pyproject.toml` (+`jinja2`) and `uv.lock`
- `src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/templates/audit_status.html.j2` (new)
- `src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/reporter_lambda.py`
- `template.yaml`

import json
import os
from datetime import datetime, timezone
from pathlib import Path
from typing import TypedDict, cast
from unittest.mock import MagicMock

from aws_lambda_powertools.utilities.typing import LambdaContext
from pytest import MonkeyPatch, mark

from dns_de_rte_orchestrator.audit_logging import (
    AUDIT_LOG_S3_BUCKET,
    AUDIT_LOG_S3_PREFIX,
    DYNAMO_DB_AUDIT_LOGGING_TABLE,
)
from dns_de_rte_orchestrator.request_repository import REPOSITORY_TYPE_ENV_VAR

# Set required env vars before reporter_lambda is imported — its module-level
# get_audit_log_reader() call needs these to be present at import time.
_ = os.environ.setdefault(DYNAMO_DB_AUDIT_LOGGING_TABLE, "test-audit-table")
_ = os.environ.setdefault(AUDIT_LOG_S3_BUCKET, "test-bucket")
_ = os.environ.setdefault(AUDIT_LOG_S3_PREFIX, "test-prefix")
_ = os.environ.setdefault(REPOSITORY_TYPE_ENV_VAR, "memory")
_ = os.environ.setdefault("AWS_DEFAULT_REGION", "eu-west-1")
_ = os.environ.setdefault("AWS_REGION", "eu-west-1")
_ = os.environ.setdefault("AWS_EC2_METADATA_DISABLED", "true")
_ = os.environ.setdefault("AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID", "test-access-key")
_ = os.environ.setdefault("AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY", "test-secret-key")


import dns_de_rte_orchestrator.reporter_lambda as reporter_module  # noqa: E402
from dns_de_rte_orchestrator.audit_logging import AuditEntry  # noqa: E402
from dns_de_rte_orchestrator.reporter_lambda import lambda_handler  # noqa: E402


class _OkBody(TypedDict):
    id: str
    entries: list[dict[str, str]]


class _NotFoundBody(TypedDict):
    error: str
    id: str


class _ErrorBody(TypedDict):
    error: str


def _parse_ok(response: dict[str, object]) -> _OkBody:
    body = response["body"]
    assert isinstance(body, str)
    return cast(_OkBody, json.loads(body))


def _parse_not_found(response: dict[str, object]) -> _NotFoundBody:
    body = response["body"]
    assert isinstance(body, str)
    return cast(_NotFoundBody, json.loads(body))


def _parse_error(response: dict[str, object]) -> _ErrorBody:
    body = response["body"]
    assert isinstance(body, str)
    return cast(_ErrorBody, json.loads(body))


class _RecordingReader:
    def __init__(self, result: list[AuditEntry] | Exception) -> None:
        self._result: list[AuditEntry] | Exception = result
        self.calls: list[str] = []

    def get_logs(self, id: str) -> list[AuditEntry]:
        self.calls.append(id)
        if isinstance(self._result, Exception):
            raise self._result
        return self._result


def make_get_event(id: str | None = "req-123") -> dict[str, object]:
    return {
        "pathParameters": {"id": id} if id is not None else None,
        "httpMethod": "GET",
    }


def make_context() -> LambdaContext:
    return cast(LambdaContext, MagicMock())


def invoke(event: dict[str, object]) -> dict[str, object]:
    # cast: powertools decorators erase the return type annotation to Any
    return cast(dict[str, object], lambda_handler(event, make_context()))


FIXTURES_DIR = Path(__file__).parent / "fixtures"
TS = datetime(2026, 5, 7, 14, 30, 45, tzinfo=timezone.utc)


def load_fixture(name: str) -> dict[str, object]:
    with (FIXTURES_DIR / name).open(encoding="utf-8") as f:
        return cast(dict[str, object], json.load(f))


def test_lambda_handler_returns_200_with_entries(monkeypatch: MonkeyPatch):
    entries = [
        AuditEntry(id="req-123", timestamp=TS, message="created"),
        AuditEntry(
            id="req-123",
            timestamp=datetime(2026, 5, 7, 15, 0, 0, tzinfo=timezone.utc),
            message="processed",
        ),
    ]
    monkeypatch.setattr(reporter_module, "reader", _RecordingReader(entries))

    response = invoke(make_get_event("req-123"))

    assert response["statusCode"] == 200
    body = _parse_ok(response)
    assert body["id"] == "req-123"
    assert len(body["entries"]) == 2
    assert body["entries"][0]["message"] == "created"
    assert body["entries"][1]["message"] == "processed"
    assert body["entries"][0]["timestamp"] == "2026-05-07T14:30:45+00:00"


@mark.parametrize(
    ("fixture_name", "erasure_id"),
    [
        ("reporter_get_status_s3_and_dynamodb.json", "ERASURE-7384"),
        ("reporter_get_status_dynamodb_only.json", "ERASURE-1437"),
        ("reporter_get_status_s3_only.json", "ERASURE-1289"),
    ],
)
def test_lambda_handler_accepts_fake_audit_log_event_fixtures(
    monkeypatch: MonkeyPatch,
    fixture_name: str,
    erasure_id: str,
):
    entries = [AuditEntry(id=erasure_id, timestamp=TS, message="fixture test")]
    reader = _RecordingReader(entries)
    monkeypatch.setattr(reporter_module, "reader", reader)

    response = invoke(load_fixture(fixture_name))

    assert response["statusCode"] == 200
    assert reader.calls == [erasure_id]
    body = _parse_ok(response)
    assert body["id"] == erasure_id


def test_lambda_handler_returns_404_for_unknown_id_fixture(
    monkeypatch: MonkeyPatch,
):
    monkeypatch.setattr(reporter_module, "reader", _RecordingReader([]))

    response = invoke(load_fixture("reporter_get_status_unknown_id.json"))

    assert response["statusCode"] == 404
    body = _parse_not_found(response)
    assert body["id"] == "ERASURE-0000"


def test_lambda_handler_returns_404_when_no_entries(monkeypatch: MonkeyPatch):
    monkeypatch.setattr(reporter_module, "reader", _RecordingReader([]))

    response = invoke(make_get_event("req-unknown"))

    assert response["statusCode"] == 404
    body = _parse_not_found(response)
    assert body["id"] == "req-unknown"
    assert "error" in body


def test_lambda_handler_returns_400_when_path_param_missing(monkeypatch: MonkeyPatch):
    monkeypatch.setattr(reporter_module, "reader", _RecordingReader([]))
    event: dict[str, object] = {"pathParameters": None}

    response = invoke(event)

    assert response["statusCode"] == 400
    body = _parse_error(response)
    assert "error" in body


def test_lambda_handler_returns_400_when_id_is_empty_string(monkeypatch: MonkeyPatch):
    monkeypatch.setattr(reporter_module, "reader", _RecordingReader([]))

    response = invoke(make_get_event("  "))

    assert response["statusCode"] == 400


def test_lambda_handler_returns_400_when_pathparameters_key_absent(
    monkeypatch: MonkeyPatch,
):
    monkeypatch.setattr(reporter_module, "reader", _RecordingReader([]))
    event: dict[str, object] = {}

    response = invoke(event)

    assert response["statusCode"] == 400


def test_lambda_handler_returns_500_on_reader_exception(monkeypatch: MonkeyPatch):
    monkeypatch.setattr(
        reporter_module, "reader", _RecordingReader(RuntimeError("dynamo down"))
    )

    response = invoke(make_get_event("req-123"))

    assert response["statusCode"] == 500
    body = _parse_error(response)
    assert body["error"] == "internal error"


def test_lambda_handler_response_has_json_content_type(monkeypatch: MonkeyPatch):
    entries = [AuditEntry(id="req-1", timestamp=TS, message="msg")]
    monkeypatch.setattr(reporter_module, "reader", _RecordingReader(entries))

    response = invoke(make_get_event("req-1"))

    assert response["headers"] == {"Content-Type": "application/json"}


def test_lambda_handler_entry_shape_matches_audit_entry_to_json(
    monkeypatch: MonkeyPatch,
):
    entry = AuditEntry(id="req-1", timestamp=TS, message="test msg")
    monkeypatch.setattr(reporter_module, "reader", _RecordingReader([entry]))

    response = invoke(make_get_event("req-1"))

    body = _parse_ok(response)
    assert body["entries"][0] == entry.to_json()

# TODO List

This file contains a checklist of things to do for the RTE Orchestration
project. It is intended to help guide human and AI agents:

- [x] Create project skeleton using `uv`
- [x] Add `README.md`
- [x] Add `AGENTS.md` and link `CLAUDE.md` to it
- [x] Set up `pre-commit` or `prek` to ensure code quality

  - [x] `basedpyright` for type checking
  - [x] `ruff` for general linting
  - [x] `black` for formatting
  - [x] Make sure all are added to prek where possible
- [x] Make sure `pytest` works
- [x] Add code coverage plugin for tests
- [x] Document how to use `uv` and other tools in README in case people are
  unfamiliar
- [x] Add type stubs for `boto3` since AWS does not provide them
- [x] Implement storage layer
  - [x] Add basic interface/abstract class for storage repository
  - [x] Add an example in-memory implementation to validate ideas and test with

  - [x] Add AWS DynamoDB implementation
    - [x] Implement converting domain object to DynamoDB request

      variables

    - [x] Tests for creating items
    - [x] Tests for updating items
    - [x] Tests for getting single items
    - [x] Tests for environment variables
    - [x] Tests for getting all entries in a given state

  - [ ] Implement business logic

- [ ] Update the Customer API code to use requests library instead of urllib

  - [x] Get SAM to not package the whole repo as an artifact to send to AWS
  - [x] Add `sam validate` step to CI/CD
  - [x] Add `sam build` step to CI/CD
  - [x] Add `sam deploy` steps to CI/CD
  - [ ] Fix `sam delete` step in CI/CD so it doesn't prompt for user input
- [x] Make sure AWS SAM deployment can be done from CI/CD to `dev` and `prod`
  - [x] Confirm AWS SAM deployment can be done to `dev` via CI/CD
  - [x] Confirm AWS SAM deployment can be done to `prod` via CI/CD
